---
bibliography: "propositional-logic.bib"
number-sections: true
crossref:
  chapters: true
---

{{< include /assets/macros/macros.qmd >}}

# Propositional Logic

{{< include introduction.qmd >}}

{{< include syntax.qmd >}}

:::{.content-hidden when-profile="lecture-6"}
{{< include semantics.qmd >}}
:::

:::{.content-hidden when-profile="lecture-6"}
:::{.content-hidden when-profile="lecture-7"}
{{< include completeness.qmd >}}
:::
:::

:::{.content-hidden when-profile="lecture-6"}
:::{.content-hidden when-profile="lecture-7"}
:::{.content-hidden when-profile="lecture-8"}
{{< include proofs.qmd >}}
:::
:::
:::

### The Rules for Disjunction

The rule for the introduction of disjunctions into proofs is straightforward:

($\vee$I): From either $\ph$ or $\psi$, you may infer the sentence $\ph\vee\psi$.

Consider, for example, the following argument:

$$ \ph, (\psi\vee\ph)\imp\chi\proves\chi $$

It's proof is as follows:

:::{.lproof}

    1.  | \varphi         [Premise]
    2.  | \psi\vee\varphi   [$\vee$I: 1]
    3.  | (\psi\vee\varphi)\rightarrow\chi     [Premise]
    4.  | \chi              [$\rightarrow$E: 2, 3]

:::

The rule for disjunction elimination is somewhat more involved:

($\vee$E): If from the hypothesis $\ph$ you can derive $\chi$ and from the
hypothesis $\psi$ you can derive $\chi$, then from $\ph\vee\psi$ you may infer
the sentence $\chi$.

This rule is intended to capture the method of reasoning by cases. The rule says
that if a certain sentence follows from either of two hypotheses, then it
follows from their disjunction. To apply this rule in the context of a proof, we
have to give two subproofs both of which conclude in the same sentence. From
these two subproofs along with the disjunction of the two hypotheses initiating
them, we may infer their shared conclusion.

Let's see how this rule can be used to validate the following argument:

$$ \ph\vee\psi, \ph\imp\chi, \psi\imp\chi \proves\chi$$

:::{.lproof}

    1.  | |_  \varphi              [Hypothesis]
    2.  | | \varphi\rightarrow\chi      [Premise]
    3.  | | \chi                        [$\rightarrow$E: 1, 2]
    4.  | |_ \psi                   [Hypothesis]
    5.  | |  \psi\rightarrow\chi        [Premise]
    6.  | | \chi                    [$\rightarrow$E: 4, 5]
    7.  | \varphi\vee\psi               [Premise]
    8.  | \chi                      [$\vee$E: 3, 6, 7]

:::

The subproof appearing in lines 1--3 of the proof establishes that $\chi$
follows from the hypothesis $\ph$. The subproof appearing in lines 4--6
establishes that $\chi$ follows from the hypothesis $\psi$. It therefore follows
that $\chi$ follows from $\ph\vee\psi$ by the rule of disjunction elimination.

Here's another example:

$$ \ph\vee\psi, \neg\ph\proves\psi$$

:::{.lproof}

    1.  | |_  \varphi              [Hypothesis]
    2.  | | |_  \neg\psi           [Hypothesis]
    3.  | | |   \varphi               [Reiteration: 1]
    4.  | | |   \neg\varphi           [Premise]
    5.  | | \neg\neg\psi              [$\neg$I: 3, 4]
    6.  | | \psi                      [$\neg$E: 5]
    7.  | |_ \psi                   [Hypothesis]
    8.  | \varphi\vee\psi               [Premise]
    9.  | \psi                      [$\vee$E: 6, 7, 8]

:::

Note that the second subproof is just one line since the hypothesis that initiates
the subproof is the conclusion that we aim to establish.

Here's one last example that is a bit more involved:

$$ \ph\vee\psi\proves\neg(\neg\ph\conj\neg\psi) $$

:::{.lproof}

    1.  \varphi\vee\psi                         [Premise]
    2.  |   |_  \varphi
    3.  |   |   |_  \neg\varphi\wedge\neg\psi
    4.  |   |   |   \neg\varphi                 [$\wedge$E: 3]
    5.  |   |   |   \varphi                     [Reiteration: 2]
    6.  |   |   \neg(\neg\varphi\wedge\neg\psi) [$\neg$I: 4, 5]
    7.  |   |_  \psi
    8.  |   |   |_  \neg\varphi\wedge\neg\psi
    9.  |   |   |   \neg\psi                    [$\wedge$E: 8]
    10. |   |   |   \psi                        [Reiteration: 7]
    11. |   |   \neg(\neg\varphi\wedge\neg\psi) [$\neg$E: 9, 10]
    12. \neg(\neg\varphi\wedge\neg\psi)         [$\vee$E: 1, 6, 11]

:::

### The Rules for the Biconditional

A biconditional may be thought of as two conjoined conditionals. After all:

$$ \ph\iff\psi \equiv (\ph\imp\psi)\conj(\psi\imp\ph)$$

Accordingly, the rules for reasoning with biconditionals are similar to those for conditionals,
only they exhibit the kind of symmetry indicated by the above equivalence. For
example the rule for biconditional elimination is:

($\iff$E): From $\ph$ and $\ph\iff\psi$, you may infer $\psi$, and from $\psi$
and $\ph\iff\psi$, you may infer $\ph$.

Here is an example of how this rule can be applied:

$$\ph\iff\psi, \ph\conj\chi \proves \psi\conj\chi$$

:::{.lproof}

    1. | \varphi\leftrightarrow\psi        [Premise]
    2. | \varphi\wedge\chi                 [Premise]
    3. | \varphi                            [$\wedge$E: 2]
    4. | \psi                               [$\leftrightarrow$E: 1, 3]
    5. | \chi                               [$\wedge$E: 2]
    6. | \psi\wedge\chi                   [$\wedge$I: 4, 5]

:::

As this last example illustrates, biconditionals license a kind of general
substitution of one term for the other. Here is another example that makes the
same point:

$$\ph\iff\psi, \ph\disj\chi \proves \psi\disj\chi$$

:::{.lproof}

    1. |  |_ \varphi         [Hypothesis]
    2. |  |  \varphi\leftrightarrow\psi    [Premise]
    3. |  |  \psi                          [$\leftrightarrow$E: 1, 2]
    4. |  |  \psi\vee\chi                 [$\vee$I: 3]
    5. |  |_ \chi           [Hypothesis]
    6. |  |  \psi\vee\chi                 [$\vee$I: 5]
    7. |  \varphi\vee\chi                 [Premise]
    8. |  \psi\vee\chi                    [$\vee$E: 4, 6, 7]

:::

The rule for biconditional introduction is as follows:

($\iff$I): If from the hypothesis $\ph$ you can derive $\psi$ and from the
hypothesis $\psi$ you can derive $\ph$, then you may infer $\ph\iff\psi$.

As was the case with the rule of disjunction elimination, the rule of
biconditional introduction requires two subproofs. Together, these two subproofs
show that one term of the biconditional can be inferred from the other and vice
versa.

Suppose, for example, we wish to establish that
$(\ph\conj\psi)\iff(\psi\conj\ph)$ is a logical truth, i.e., that the
following argument is valid:

$$\proves(\ph\conj\psi)\iff(\psi\conj\ph)$$

The proof is as follows:

:::{.lproof}

    1. |  |_ \varphi\wedge\psi        [Hypothesis]
    2. |  |  \varphi    [$\wedge$E: 1]
    3. |  |  \psi                          [$\wedge$E: 1]
    4. |  |  \psi\wedge\varphi             [$\wedge$I: 2, 3]
    5. |  |_ \psi\wedge\varphi        [Hypothesis]
    6. |  |  \psi    [$\wedge$E: 5]
    7. |  |  \varphi                          [$\wedge$E: 5]
    8. |  |  \varphi\wedge\psi             [$\wedge$I: 6, 7]
    9. |  (\varphi\wedge\psi)\leftrightarrow(\psi\wedge\varphi)   [$\leftrightarrow$I: 4, 8]

:::

Together, the rules for negation, conjunction, disjunction, conditionals and
biconditionals allow us to give proofs of every valid argument in the language
of propositional logic.

:::{#exr-proofs-in-p-2 .exercise}
{{< include /content/exercises/proofs-in-p-2.qmd >}}
:::

## References {.unnumbered}
